content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 19,
1996
Gary M.
Baum
City of Morgan Hill
17555 Peak Avenue
Morgan Hill,
CA
95037
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1-96-202
Dear Mr.
Baum:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”)
1 as they pertain to City of Morgan Hill Mayor John Varela.
2
Since your request does not pertain to a specific governmental
decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor
with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
QUESTION
Will the mayor have a conflict of interest in decisions
involving merchants who have business before the city council and
who also are sources of income to him?
CONCLUSION
The mayor may not participate in any governmental decision
initiated by a source of income to him, or in any proceeding in
which a source of income is a named party or the subject of the
proceeding.
In addition, the mayor may not participate in any
decision that will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
a source of income indirectly.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
Commission regulations
appear at Title 2,
Sections 18000—18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.
2
The identity of the requestor was provided by you in a
conversation with Steven Churchwell on July 18,
1996.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—96—202
Page
2
FACTS
The mayor of the City of Morgan Hill is a partner with at
least a 10 percent ownership share in a business.
3
The business
involves an outdoor market which operates one day a week.
The
primary revenue to the mayor’s business is from stall or space
rental at the market.
The rental in some instances will exceed
$250 over a one year period.
The possibility exists that local
merchants who have other business before the city council may
become vendors at the site.
You inquire as to what precautions
should be taken to avoid any potential conflicts of interest in
operating the business.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Financial Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on, inter
alia,
“any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250>
or
more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.”
(Section 87103(c).)
Your facts indicate that the mayor anticipates that in some
instances rental income will exceed $250 in a 12-month period.
Thus,
in that instance, the tenant would become a source of income
to the mayor pursuant to Section 87103(c).
You state that the
mayor is a partner in the business and has at least a 10 percent
share in the business.
Since the mayor owns at least a 10 percent
share of the business,
sources of income to the business (i.e.,
the tenants)
are considered sources of income to the mayor as
well.
(Section 82030.)
Therefore, the mayor may not participate
in any governmental decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the business itself or
sources of income to the business of over $250 in a
12—month..
period.
The fact pattern in your letter is prospective in nature.
However, in your conversation with Steven Churchwell,
you informed
him that the mayor’s business is currently “in business.”


Our File No.
1-96-202
Page
3
Foreseeabi 1ity
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required,
however, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
You state that it is foreseeable that income in excess of
$250 in a 12-month period will be received by the mayor for space
rental from certain tenants of the market.
Materiality
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which
provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial
effects of a decision are material.
These regulations apply
different standards depending on whether the decision will
directly or indirectly affect the official’s economic interest.
Your letter does not refer to a specific governmental decision.
Therefore, the discussion below includes the appropriate guidance
for decisions where the mayor may be either directly or indirectly
affected.
4
Regulation 18702.1(b) provides as follows:
(b)
A person or business entity is directly
involved in a decision before an official’s agency when
that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in, or is the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency.
Your letter does not provide information regarding the size of
the mayor’s business entity.
However, for your information,
attached is Regulation 18703.5 which discusses an exception for
sources of income to owners of retail business entities.
The
determination of whether or not this regulation will apply is a
factual one depending on whether the criteria set out in the
regulation is met.


Our File No.
1—96-202
Page
4
(3)
A person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to,
or contract with, the
subject person or business entity..
***
(Emphasis added.)
If the mayor’s business or any source of income to the
business is directly involved in a decision before the city
council, the mayor would be required to disqualify himself from
the decision.
If the person or business entity that is the source of income
to the public official is not directly involved the decision, then
the public official must refer to additional Commission
regulations to determine materiality.
If the source of income is
an individual, then the effect of the decision will be material if
any of the following applies:
(a)
The decision will affect the individual’s
income,
investments,
or other tangible or intangible
assets or liabilities (other than real property by
$1,000 or more; or
(b)
The decision will affect the individual’s real
property interest in a manner that is considered
material under Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.
(Regulation 18702.6.)
Thus, if any decision the city council makes has the above
described effect on the mayor’s business or sources of income to
the business, then the mayor must disqualify himself from that
decision.
As to the effect of a decision on a business tenant,
you must
refer to Regulation 18702.2.
For a relatively small business,
Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that the effect is material if:
(1)
The decision will result ‘in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.


Our File No.
1—96-202
Page 5
Accordingly, if any decision has the above-described effect
on the mayor’s business or sources of income to the business, the
mayor must disqualify himself from the decision.
If you have any
further questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
ynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

a source of income indirectly.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will the mayor have a conflict of interest in decisions
involving merchants who have business before the city council and
who also are sources of income to him?

CONCLUSION: The mayor may not participate in any governmental decision
initiated by a source of income to him, or in any proceeding in
which a source of income is a named party or the subject of the
proceeding.
In addition, the mayor may not participate in any
decision that will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
a source of income indirectly.
2
The identity of the requestor was provided by you in a
conversation with Steven Churchwell on July 18,
1996.

(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—96—202
